audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man phoning the customer services manager of a hotel to get information about holding a conference there.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Flanders conference hotel.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Oh, hi, I wanted to ask about conference facilities at the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Have I come through to the right person?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. You have.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: I'm the customer services manager.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: My name's Angela.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: The name of the customer services manager is Angela.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So Angela has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Flanders conference hotel.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Oh, hi, I wanted to ask about conference facilities at the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Have I come through to the right person?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. You have.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: I'm the customer services manager.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: My name's Angela.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: So, how can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Well, I'm calling from Barrett and Stansons.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We're a medical company based in Perth.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: And we're organizing a conference for our clients to be held in Sydney.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It'll be held over two days, and we're expecting about 50 or 60 people.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: When were you thinking of having it?
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Sometime early next year, like the end of January, it would have to be a weekend.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Let me see.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Our conference facilities are already booked for the weekend beginning January the 28th.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: We could do the first weekend in February.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: How about January the 21st?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Ah, oh, I'm afraid that's booked too.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Well, let's go for the February date then.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So, that's the weekend beginning the 4th.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: OK, now can you tell me a bit about what conference facilities you have?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Sure, uh, so for talks and presentations, we have the Tesla room.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Sorry?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Tesla, that's spelled TESLA.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: It holds up to 100 people, and it's fully equipped with a projector and so on.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: How about a microphone?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that'll be all set up ready for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: And there'll be one that members of the audience can use too, for questions if necessary.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And we'll also need some sort of open area where people can sit and have a cup of coffee.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And we'd like to have an exhibition of our products and services there as well, so that'll need to be quite a big space.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. That's fine.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: There's a central atrium with all those facilities, and you can come before the conference starts if you want to set everything up.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Great, and I presume there's wifi.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: That's free and available throughout the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: OK, would you also like us to provide a buffet lunch?
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: We can do a two course meal with a number of different options.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: What sort of price are we looking at for that?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I can send you a copy of the standard menu.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: That's $45 per person, or you can have the special for $25 more.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: I think the standard should be OK, but yes, send me the menu.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, we're also going to need accommodation on the Saturday night for some of the participants.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure how many, but probably about 25 so, what do you charge for a room?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, for conference attendees, we have a 25% reduction.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: So we can offer you rooms at $135.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Normally a standard room's $180.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: And does that include breakfast?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Sure, and of course guests can also make use of all the other facilities at the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: So we've got a spa where you can get massages and facials and so on.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: And there's a pool up on the roof for the use of guests.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Oh. Great.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now what about transport links?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The hotels downtown, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's about 12 km from the airport, but there's a complimentary shuttle bus for guests, and it's only about 10 minutes' walk from the Central Railway station.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now, I don't know Sydney very well.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Can you just give me an idea of the location of the hotel?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Well, it's downtown on Wilby Street.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Uh. That's quite a small street, and it's not very far from the sea.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: And of course, if the conference attendees want to go out on their Saturday evening, there's a huge choice of places to eat.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: Then, if they want to make a night of it, they can go on to one of the clubs in the area.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: There are a great many to choose from.
audioscript.speaker: ANGELA

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So, if we go ahead with this, can you give me some information about how much we're looking at?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: An Introduction to Volunteering and Its Impact

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman talking to a group of college students about volunteering to help other people.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, my name's Lucy Crittenden, and I'm the director of operations for an organization that arranges volunteering in this part of the country.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: I'm hoping I can persuade one or two of you to become volunteers yourselves.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Let me start by briefly explaining what we mean by volunteering.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Volunteers are teenagers and adults who choose to spend some time unpaid, helping other people in some way.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Most volunteers devote two or three hours to this every week.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: While a few do much more, the people they help may have physical or behavioral difficulties, for example. Volunteers can do all sorts of things, depending on their own abilities and interests.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: If they're supporting a family that's struggling for example, they may be able to give them tips on cooking.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Or recommend how to plan their budget, or how to shop sensibly on their income.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: They might even do some painting or wallpapering, perhaps alongside any members of the family.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Who are able to do it, or even do some babysitting, so that parents can go out for a while.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: The benefit from volunteering isn't only for the people being helped, volunteers also gain from it.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: They're using their skills to cope with somebody's mental or physical ill health.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: And volunteering may be a valuable element of their CV when they're applying for jobs.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Employers usually look favorably on someone who's given up time to help others.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Significantly, most volunteers feel that what they're doing gives them a purpose in their lives, and in my opinion, they're lucky in that respect.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: As many people don't have that feeling.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, I'd like to tell you what some of our volunteers have said about what they do, to give you an idea of the range of ways in which they can help people.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Habib supports an elderly lady who's beginning to show signs of dementia.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Once a week, they along with other elderly people, go to the local community center, where a group of people come in and sing.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: The songs take the listeners back to their youth.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: And for a little while, they can forget the difficulties that they face now.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Our volunteer Consuela is an amazing woman.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: She has difficulty walking herself.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: But she doesn't let that stop her.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: She helps a couple of people with similar difficulties, who had almost stopped walking altogether, by using herself as an example.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Consuela encourages them to walk more and more.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Minh visits a young man who lives alone, and can't leave his home on his own.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: So he hardly ever saw anyone, but together they go out to the cinema.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Or to see friends, the young man hadn't been able to visit for a long time.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Tanya visits an elderly woman once a week.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: When the woman found out that Tanya is a professional dressmaker.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: She got interested.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Tanya showed her some soft toys she'd made, and the woman decided to try it herself.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: And now she really enjoys it, and spends hours making toys.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: They're not perhaps up to Tanya's standard yet, but she gains a lot of pleasure from doing it.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Alexei is a volunteer with a family that faces a number of difficulties.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: By calmly talking over possible solutions with family members, he's helping them to realize that they aren't helpless, and that they can do something themselves to improve their situation.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: This has been great for their self-esteem, and the last volunteer I'll mention, though there are plenty more, is Juba.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: She volunteers with a teenage girl with learning difficulties.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Who wasn't very good at talking to other people.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Juba's worked very patiently with her, and now the girl is far better at expressing herself, and at understanding other people.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: OK, I hope that's given you an idea of what volunteering is all about.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: Now I'd like to talk to you about...
audioscript.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: They're using their skills to cope with somebody's mental or physical ill health.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: And volunteering may be a valuable element of their CV when they're applying for jobs.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Employers usually look favorably on someone who's given up time to help others.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Significantly, most volunteers feel that what they're doing gives them a purpose in their lives, and in my opinion, they're lucky in that respect.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: As many people don't have that feeling.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, I'd like to tell you what some of our volunteers have said about what they do, to give you an idea of the range of ways in which they can help people.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Habib supports an elderly lady who's beginning to show signs of dementia.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Once a week, they along with other elderly people, go to the local community center, where a group of people come in and sing.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: The songs take the listeners back to their youth.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: And for a little while, they can forget the difficulties that they face now.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Our volunteer Consuela is an amazing woman.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: She has difficulty walking herself.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: But she doesn't let that stop her.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: She helps a couple of people with similar difficulties, who had almost stopped walking altogether, by using herself as an example.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Consuela encourages them to walk more and more.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Minh visits a young man who lives alone, and can't leave his home on his own.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: So he hardly ever saw anyone, but together they go out to the cinema.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Or to see friends, the young man hadn't been able to visit for a long time.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Tanya visits an elderly woman once a week.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: When the woman found out that Tanya is a professional dressmaker.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: She got interested.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Tanya showed her some soft toys she'd made, and the woman decided to try it herself.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: And now she really enjoys it, and spends hours making toys.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: They're not perhaps up to Tanya's standard yet, but she gains a lot of pleasure from doing it.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Alexei is a volunteer with a family that faces a number of difficulties.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: By calmly talking over possible solutions with family members, he's helping them to realize that they aren't helpless, and that they can do something themselves to improve their situation.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: This has been great for their self-esteem, and the last volunteer I'll mention, though there are plenty more, is Juba.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: She volunteers with a teenage girl with learning difficulties.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Who wasn't very good at talking to other people.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Juba's worked very patiently with her, and now the girl is far better at expressing herself, and at understanding other people.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: OK, I hope that's given you an idea of what volunteering is all about.
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: Now I'd like to talk to you about...
utterances.speaker: LUCY CRITTENDEN

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO activities that volunteers do are mentioned?
Which TWO ways that volunteers can benefit from volunteering are mentioned?
options: learning how to be part of a team

text: What has each of the following volunteers helped someone to do?
textReadable: What has each of the following volunteers helped someone to do?

list: rediscover skills not used for a long time
body.title: Volunteers
body.listTitle: What volunteers have helped people to do

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a trainee music teacher called Joe talking to his supervisor about the school marching band that he has been given responsibility for.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, how are you getting on with your teaching practice at the high school, Joe?
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: Well, I've been put in charge of the school marching band, and it's quite a responsibility.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I'd like to talk it over with you.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Go ahead.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: You'd better start by giving me a bit of background.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Well, the band has students in it from all years.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: So they're aged 11 to 18, and there are about 50 of them altogether.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: It's quite a popular activity within the school.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I've never worked with a band of more than 20 before, and this is very different.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I can imagine.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: They aren't really good enough to enter national band competitions, but they're in a regional one later in the term.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Even if they don't win, and I don't expect them to.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Hopefully it'll be an incentive for them to try and improve.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Yes, hopefully.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: Well, now the town council's organizing a carnival in the summer.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: And the band has been asked to perform.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: If you ask me, they aren't really up to it yet, and I need to get them functioning better as a band, and in a very short time.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Have you been doing anything with them?
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: Apart from practicing the music, I mean.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: I played a recording I came across of a drummer talking about how playing in a band had changed his life.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I think it was an after-dinner speech.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I thought it was pretty inspiring, because being in the band had stopped him from getting involved in crime.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: The students seemed to find it interesting too.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: That's good.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: I'm planning to show them that old film from the 1940s 'Strike Up the Band', and talk about it with the students.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: What do you think?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: As it's about a school band, it might make the students realize how much they can achieve if they work together.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: That's what I've got in mind.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I'm hoping I can take some of the band to a parade that's going to take place next month.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: A couple of marching bands will be performing, and the atmosphere should be quite exciting.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: It depends on whether I can persuade the school to hire a coach or two to take us there.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Mmm. They sound like good ideas to me.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Can I tell you about a few people in the band who I'm finding it quite difficult to cope with?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I'm sure you'll have some ideas about what I can do.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Go ahead.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: There's a flautist who says she loves playing in the band.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: We rehearse twice a week after school, but she's hardly ever there.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Then she looks for me the next day, and gives me a very plausible reason.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: She says she had to help her mother, or she's been ill, but to be honest, I don't believe her.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Oh, dear.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: Any more students with difficulties?
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: Plenty.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Uh. There's a trumpeter who thinks she's the best musician in the band, though she certainly isn't.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: She's always saying what she thinks other people should do, which makes my job pretty difficult.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: She sounds a bit of a nightmare.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: You can say that again.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Uh. One of the trombonists has got an impressive sense of rhythm, and could be an excellent musician.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Except that he has breathing difficulties, and he doesn't really have enough breath for the trombone.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: He'd be much better off playing percussion for instance, but he refuses to give up, so he ends up only playing half the notes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I suppose you have to admire his determination.
audioscript.speaker: LIZZIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Maybe. One of the percussionists isn't too bad, but he never seems to interact with other people.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: And he always rushes off as soon as the rehearsal ends.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I don't know if there are family reasons or what, but it isn't good in a band where people really need to feel they're part of a group.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Hmm. There are others too, but at least that gives you an idea of what I'm up against.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Do you have any thoughts about what I can do, Lizzie?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Band members

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a British university lecturer in music talking about concerts in an arts festival.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As you all know, the university is planning an arts festival for later this year, and here in the music department, we've planned three concerts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These will be public performances, and the program has just been finalized.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The theme of the festival is links between the UK and Australia.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this is reflected in the music.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Each concert will feature both British and Australian composers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'll tell you briefly about the Australian music.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you probably won't be familiar with that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The first concert will include music by Liza Lim, who was born in Perth, Western Australia in 1966.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As a child Lim originally learned to play the piano like so many children, and also the violin.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But when she was 11, her teachers encouraged her to start composing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: She found this was her real strength, and she studied and later taught composition, both in Australia and in other countries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As a composer, she has received commissions from numerous orchestras, other performers, and festivals in several countries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Liza Lim's compositions are vibrant and full of energy, and she often explores Asian and Australian Aboriginal cultural sources.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Including the native instrument the didgeridoo.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is featured in a work called The Compass.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Her music is very expressive.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So although it is complex, it has the power of connecting with audiences and performers alike.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the festival, we're going to give a semi-staged performance of The Oresteia.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is an opera in seven parts, based on the trilogy of ancient Greek tragedies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By Aeschylus.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Lim composed this when she was in her mid twenties, and she also wrote the text along with Barrie Kosky.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's performed by six singers, a dancer, and an orchestra that, as well as standard orchestral instruments, includes electric guitar.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And a traditional Turkish stringed instrument.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Lim wrote that, because the stories in the tragedies are not easy to tell.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The sounds she creates are also disturbing, and they include breathing, sobbing, laughing, and whistling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The work lasts around 75 minutes, and the rest of the concert will consist of orchestral works by the British composers Ralph Vaughan Williams.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And Frederick Delius.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Moving on now to our second concert, this will begin with instrumental music by British composers, Benjamin Britten and Judith Weir.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After the interval, we'll go to Australia for a piece by Ross Edwards, The Tower of Remoteness, according to Edwards.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The inspiration for this piece came from nature, when he was sitting alone in the dry bed of a creek, overshadowed by the leaves of palm trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Listening to the birds and insects.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Tower of Remoteness is scored for piano and clarinet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Edwards says he realized years after writing the piece.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That he had subconsciously modeled its opening phrase on a bird call.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ross Edwards was born in 1943 in Sydney, Australia, and studied at the Sydney Conservatorium of Music, and the universities of Adelaide and Sydney.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He's well known in Australia, and in fact, he's one of the country's most performed composers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He's written a wide range of music, from symphonies and concertos to some composed specifically for children.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Edward's music has been described as being 'deeply connected to Australia', and it can be regarded as a Celebration of the diversity of cultures that Australia can be proud of.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The last of the three Australian composers to be represented in our festival is Carl Vine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Born in 1954 Vine, like Liza Lim, comes from Perth, Western Australia.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He took up the cornet at the age of five, switching to the piano five years later.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, he went to university to study physics before changing to composition.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After graduating, he moved to Sydney.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And worked as a freelance pianist and composer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before long, he had become prominent in Australia as a composer for dance, and in fact has written 25 scores of that type.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In our third concert, Vine will be represented by his music for the flag handover ceremony of the Olympics held in 1996. This seven-minute orchestral piece was of course heard by millions of people worldwide.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And we'll hear it alongside works written by British composers, Edward Elgar, and more recently, Thomas Adès.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: As you all know, the university is planning an arts festival for later this year, and here in the music department, we've planned three concerts.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These will be public performances, and the program has just been finalized.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The theme of the festival is links between the UK and Australia.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And this is reflected in the music.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Each concert will feature both British and Australian composers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'll tell you briefly about the Australian music.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As you probably won't be familiar with that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The first concert will include music by Liza Lim, who was born in Perth, Western Australia in 1966.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As a child Lim originally learned to play the piano like so many children, and also the violin.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But when she was 11, her teachers encouraged her to start composing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: She found this was her real strength, and she studied and later taught composition, both in Australia and in other countries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As a composer, she has received commissions from numerous orchestras, other performers, and festivals in several countries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Liza Lim's compositions are vibrant and full of energy, and she often explores Asian and Australian Aboriginal cultural sources.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Including the native instrument the didgeridoo.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is featured in a work called The Compass.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Her music is very expressive.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So although it is complex, it has the power of connecting with audiences and performers alike.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the festival, we're going to give a semi-staged performance of The Oresteia.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is an opera in seven parts, based on the trilogy of ancient Greek tragedies.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By Aeschylus.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Lim composed this when she was in her mid twenties, and she also wrote the text along with Barrie Kosky.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's performed by six singers, a dancer, and an orchestra that, as well as standard orchestral instruments, includes electric guitar.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And a traditional Turkish stringed instrument.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Lim wrote that, because the stories in the tragedies are not easy to tell.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The sounds she creates are also disturbing, and they include breathing, sobbing, laughing, and whistling.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The work lasts around 75 minutes, and the rest of the concert will consist of orchestral works by the British composers Ralph Vaughan Williams.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And Frederick Delius.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Moving on now to our second concert, this will begin with instrumental music by British composers, Benjamin Britten and Judith Weir.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After the interval, we'll go to Australia for a piece by Ross Edwards, The Tower of Remoteness, according to Edwards.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The inspiration for this piece came from nature, when he was sitting alone in the dry bed of a creek, overshadowed by the leaves of palm trees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Listening to the birds and insects.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Tower of Remoteness is scored for piano and clarinet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Edwards says he realized years after writing the piece.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That he had subconsciously modeled its opening phrase on a bird call.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ross Edwards was born in 1943 in Sydney, Australia, and studied at the Sydney Conservatorium of Music, and the universities of Adelaide and Sydney.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He's well known in Australia, and in fact, he's one of the country's most performed composers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He's written a wide range of music, from symphonies and concertos to some composed specifically for children.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Edward's music has been described as being 'deeply connected to Australia', and it can be regarded as a Celebration of the diversity of cultures that Australia can be proud of.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The last of the three Australian composers to be represented in our festival is Carl Vine.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Born in 1954 Vine, like Liza Lim, comes from Perth, Western Australia.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He took up the cornet at the age of five, switching to the piano five years later.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, he went to university to study physics before changing to composition.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After graduating, he moved to Sydney.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And worked as a freelance pianist and composer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before long, he had become prominent in Australia as a composer for dance, and in fact has written 25 scores of that type.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In our third concert, Vine will be represented by his music for the flag handover ceremony of the Olympics held in 1996. This seven-minute orchestral piece was of course heard by millions of people worldwide.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And we'll hear it alongside works written by British composers, Edward Elgar, and more recently, Thomas Adès.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
